metadata.description: General non-negotiation active_listening pairs for probing only. Tests whether the active_listening vector generalises beyond negotiation contexts to everyday situations requiring active listening.
metadata.note: Contexts deliberately avoid negotiation, pricing, contracts, and business deals.

dimensions.name: Active Listening
dimensions.description: Demonstrating genuine attention by reflecting back, summarising, clarifying what was said before responding

pairs.context: A friend has explained a complicated falling-out with another mutual friend, covering several incidents over several months.
pairs.positive: So the way you see it, there wasn't one big moment — it built up over several incidents where you felt dismissed, and the thing last week was more the final straw than the actual cause. Is that how it feels?
pairs.negative: That sounds really frustrating. Have you thought about talking to them directly about it?

pairs.context: A student explains to their teacher why they didn't submit an assignment, mentioning a family situation, a technical problem, and feeling overwhelmed.
pairs.positive: Let me make sure I understand — there were three things happening at once: something difficult at home, a technical issue with the submission, and you were already feeling overwhelmed before any of that happened. Is all of that still going on?
pairs.negative: I hear you. Please submit it as soon as you can and we'll see what we can do about the late penalty given the circumstances.

pairs.context: A partner explains they have been feeling disconnected in the relationship and gives examples of feeling overlooked in small everyday ways.
pairs.positive: What I'm hearing is that it's not one big thing — it's a pattern of small moments where you haven't felt seen, and over time that has created distance. Is that how it's felt for you?
pairs.negative: I'm sorry you've been feeling that way. Let's make more effort to spend quality time together. Maybe we could plan a trip or a regular date night.

pairs.context: A colleague explains why a project fell behind schedule, mentioning unclear requirements, changing priorities from management, and a team member leaving.
pairs.positive: So there were three things stacked against you from the start — the brief kept shifting, someone left mid-project, and management kept reprioritising. Of those, which one caused the most disruption?
pairs.negative: These things happen. We'll need to figure out how to get back on track. What resources do you need to accelerate from here?

pairs.context: A teenager tells their parent they don't want to go to university and explains their reasons, including not knowing what they want to study and wanting to earn money.
pairs.positive: So it's two things — you're not ready to commit to a subject you're not sure about, and you'd rather be doing something practical and earning. Have I understood that right?
pairs.negative: A lot of people feel that way at your age. University is still worth it in the long run. What subjects are you most interested in?

pairs.context: A patient explains their symptoms to a doctor in a disorganised way, jumping between different issues and time periods.
pairs.positive: Let me play that back so I can make sure I haven't missed anything — it sounds like the main issue started about three weeks ago, you've had some related symptoms on and off before that, and there's also a separate thing with your sleep that you mentioned. Is that everything?
pairs.negative: Okay. Let's focus on the most acute issue first. When did you first notice the primary symptom?

pairs.context: A friend is describing a conflict with their manager that has made them consider resigning, giving multiple reasons.
pairs.positive: From what you're describing, it sounds like this isn't just about the one incident — it's about a longer pattern where you've felt undervalued and micromanaged, and the incident last week crystallised it. Is that fair?
pairs.negative: That sounds really difficult. Have you thought about talking to HR or looking for other roles internally first?

pairs.context: A child is explaining why they are upset about a situation at school, jumping between topics and getting emotional.
pairs.positive: I want to make sure I understand what happened. It sounds like first there was something at lunch, and then something different happened later in class, and they're both upsetting you but for different reasons. Is that right?
pairs.negative: I can see you're upset. Take a deep breath and start from the beginning so I can understand what happened.

pairs.context: An elderly relative explains at length why they are reluctant to move to assisted living, covering independence, memories, and not wanting to be a burden.
pairs.positive: What I'm hearing is that this isn't just about the practical things — it's about your independence, what the house means to you, and not wanting to feel like you're giving something up. Am I understanding that correctly?
pairs.negative: I understand it's a big change. But the support you'd get there would be really good for you. Lots of people find it much better than they expected.

pairs.context: A colleague shares a long complaint about the office environment, mixing practical issues with interpersonal tensions.
pairs.positive: Let me see if I've got all of it — there are some practical things like the noise and the hot-desking, but there's also something about the dynamic with a specific person that's making it harder to focus. Those are quite different problems. Which is bothering you more?
pairs.negative: Office environments can be challenging. Have you raised any of this with the facilities team or your manager yet?

pairs.context: A friend who has just returned from a trip explains why it was disappointing, mentioning weather, bad accommodation, and travel problems.
pairs.positive: So it was three separate things that combined — the weather spoiled the outdoor plans, the place you stayed was not what was advertised, and then getting back was a nightmare too. That's a lot of bad luck stacked up.
pairs.negative: That's a shame. Did you leave a review for the accommodation? It might help other people avoid the same experience.

pairs.context: A mentee explains they are struggling to balance their career ambitions with their current role and sounds confused about what they actually want.
pairs.positive: It sounds like there are two tensions here — part of you wants to push ahead quickly, but another part isn't sure the direction you're pushing toward is actually right for you. Is it more about the pace, or the direction?
pairs.negative: That's a really common feeling at your career stage. It might help to write down your priorities and what you want your career to look like in five years.

pairs.context: A student gives a long confused explanation to their supervisor about what their dissertation argument is.
pairs.positive: Let me try to reflect back what I heard — it sounds like the core claim is about X, supported by Y and Z, but you're not yet sure how to deal with the counterargument from the literature. Is that where you're stuck?
pairs.negative: I think you need to tighten up the argument. Go away and write a one-paragraph summary of your thesis and we'll look at it next time.

pairs.context: A parent explains to a teacher why their child has been struggling this term, mentioning home changes, friendship issues, and a possible learning difficulty.
pairs.positive: So there are potentially three things going on simultaneously — changes at home, something happening in the friendship group, and the possibility of an underlying learning difficulty. That's a lot for one child to deal with at once. What do you think is having the biggest impact?
pairs.negative: Thank you for sharing this. We'll keep a closer eye on them and I'll flag it to the SENCO in case there are additional support needs.

pairs.context: A team member explains why they feel their contribution to a successful project hasn't been recognised, giving specific examples.
pairs.positive: I want to make sure I've really heard what you're saying — it's not just about the recognition itself, it's that specific things you did were attributed to others, and that's happened more than once. Is that what's made this feel so frustrating?
pairs.negative: Recognition can be uneven in big teams — it's not always intentional. Let's make sure your contributions are documented so they're visible going forward.
